summary: Jeff Atwood responds to developer outcry over Microsoft's decision not to include refactoring tools in VB.NET 2005. He argues that automatic refactoring tools are overvalued, that refactoring requires deep code understanding that can't be captured in menu items, and that third-party tools will likely outperform Microsoft's anyway. He criticizes developers who claim they can't be professional without IDE features, arguing that tools don't make better developers—experience does.
key_points: Automatic refactoring tools can't capture the deep understanding required for meaningful refactoring
Refactoring is a pure IDE task easily implemented as add-ons, making Microsoft's built-in version unnecessary
Third-party vendors focused solely on refactoring will likely produce superior tools than Microsoft's checkbox feature
Claiming you need specific IDE features to be a professional developer is misguided
Tools may increase productivity but won't help with the harder problems of software design and understanding
money_quotes: I'd question the competency of any developer who required a tool to perform these bread and butter coding tasks efficiently.
Refactoring is just a bullet point on a box for MS. Third parties bet their entire companies on refactoring add-ins. Who do you think is going to have the better product?
Implying that the lack of refactoring support in your IDE somehow keeps you from being a professional developer is just plain stupid.
You want to be a professional developer? Stop worrying about the tools and write some damn code.
They may help me be a more productive coder, but they won't help me write better software.
Ultimately, tools don't make you a better developer. Experience does.
key_insight: Refactoring tools can make you more productive, but the hard work of understanding code, designing abstractions, and making good decisions happens in your head, not your IDE.
